parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I'm embarrassed that this thing took me a while. I feel like I should be better at this.
turns.model_response: It’s completely okay to take your time with something—everyone learns and processes things at their own pace. Being patient with yourself is important, and it’s brave to acknowledge how you’re feeling. Remember, growth often happens in those moments when things don’t come easily. You’re doing your best, and that’s what matters.

turns.user_message: I keep second-guessing everything I do at work because I'm sure I'm getting it wrong. It's like I can't trust my own judgment.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really tough. It’s completely normal to feel uncertain sometimes, especially when you care about doing a good job. But when that doubt starts to take over, it can be exhausting and make it hard to trust yourself.

Have you noticed if there’s a particular kind of task or situation that triggers these feelings the most? Sometimes pinpointing the source can help you address it more directly.

Also, remember that everyone makes mistakes—it’s part of learning and growing. If you’re open to it, you could try talking to a trusted colleague or supervisor for feedback. Sometimes an outside perspective can help you see that you’re doing better than you think.

How long have you been feeling this way?

turns.user_message: I honestly don't know how I still have my job because I mess up constantly. I feel like everyone's just waiting for me to fail.
turns.model_response:
That sounds incredibly stressful and isolating. It's really tough to feel like you're under a microscope, especially when you're already being hard on yourself. Can you share a bit more about what makes you feel like you're "messing up constantly"? Sometimes our own perception of our mistakes can be harsher than how others actually see them.

Also, have you gotten any direct feedback from supervisors or colleagues about your performance? Sometimes that can help clarify whether this is more about internal pressure or if there are specific areas to work on. Either way, it's okay to struggle—most people do at some point, even if they don't show it.
